question: Does interleukin-1 beta induce interleukin-6 mRNA expression and protein production in synovial cells from human temporomandibular joint?
ground_truth: IL-1 beta increased IL-6 production in synovial cells resulting from an increase in IL-6 mRNA expression. Enhanced production of IL-6, which is associated with bone resorption and inflammatory response, seems to be related to the progression of TMJ disorders.
baseline_prediction: The answer to the question above would be 'yes'. Interleukin-1 beta induces interleukin-6 mRNA expression and protein production in synovial cells from human temporomandibular joint. Interleukin-1 beta (IL-1 beta) is a cytokine that plays a key role in the pathogenesis of various inflammatory diseases. It is produced by many cell types, including activated T cells, macrophages, and dendritic cells. IL-1 beta has been implicated in the development of temporomandibular joint (TMJ) inflammation, which is a common cause of pain and dysfunction of the jaw. Synovial cells are the cells that line the synovial membrane of the TMJ, and they are known to produce cytokines. The production of IL-1 beta by synovial cells has been shown to be induced by IL-1 beta itself. This positive feedback loop between IL-1 beta and IL-6 is likely to contribute to the development of TMJ inflammation.

No, interleukin-1 beta does not induce interleukin-6 mRNA expression and protein production in synovial cells from human temporomandibular joint.

Explanation:
Interleukin-1 beta (IL-1β) is a pro-inflammatory cytokine that plays a key role in the pathogenesis of various diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis (RA). IL-1β is produced by synovial cells, which are located in the joint cavity and synovial membrane. These cells are responsible for the initiation and maintenance of inflammation in RA. Therefore, it is important to understand the regulation of IL-1β production by synovial cells.

One of the important factors that regulate IL-1β production is interleukin-6 (IL-6). IL-6 is a pleiotropic cytokine that is produced by various cell types, including synovial cells. IL-6 has been shown to play a key role in the pathogenesis of RA. In fact, IL-6 has been found to be elevated in the serum and synovial fluid of RA patients.

In this study, we investigated the effect of IL-1β on IL-6 mRNA expression and protein production in synovial cells from human temporomandibular joint (TMJ). We isolated and cultured synovial cells from TMJ specimens obtained from patients who underwent temporomandibular joint replacement surgery. The cells were then stimulated with IL-1β and IL-6 mRNA expression and protein production were assessed using quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), respectively.

Our results showed that IL-1β induced IL-6 mRNA expression and protein production in synovial cells from TMJ. However, the magnitude of IL-6 mRNA expression and protein production was lower than that of IL-6 mRNA expression and protein production in synovial cells from RA patients. This suggests that the regulation of IL-6 production by synovial cells from TMJ may be different from that of synovial cells from RA patients.

In conclusion, our results indicate that IL-1β induces IL-6 mRNA expression and protein production in synovial cells from TMJ. However, the magnitude of IL-6 production is lower than that of synovial cells from RA patients. This suggests that the regulation of IL-6 production by synovial cells from TMJ may be different from that of synovial cells

question: Does neointimal hyperplasia rapidly reach steady state in a novel murine vein graft model?
ground_truth: In syngeneic murine carotid interposition IVC grafts implanted with end-to-side anastomoses, moderate, nonocclusive neointimal hyperplasia reaches steady state after the fourth postoperative week. This neointimal hyperplasia, like that of human grafts, predominates near vein graft anastomoses. This vein graft model should facilitate genetic analyses of the pathogenesis of neointimal hyperplasia.

question: Is tCF7L2 rs7903146 polymorphism associated with gastric cancer : A case-control study in the Venezuelan population?
ground_truth: TCF7L2 rs7903146 polymorphism is associated with gastric cancer risk in the Venezuelan population, and could be related to determine the degree of differentiation of tumor cells.
baseline_prediction:
The aim of this study was to determine if the rs7903146 polymorphism in the TCF7L2 gene is associated with gastric cancer in the Venezuelan population.

Gastric cancer is a common malignancy worldwide, with a high prevalence in Venezuela. The TCF7L2 gene is associated with type 2 diabetes and obesity, both of which are risk factors for gastric cancer. However, the association between TCF7L2 polymorphisms and gastric cancer is not well established. In this case-control study, we genotyped the rs7903146 polymorphism in 200 patients with gastric cancer and 200 healthy controls from the Venezuelan population. We used the χ2 test to assess the association between the rs7903146 polymorphism and gastric cancer.

We found that the rs7903146 polymorphism was not associated with gastric cancer in the Venezuelan population (p=0.13). However, we did observe a significant association between the rs7903146 polymorphism and gastric cancer in a subset of Venezuelan patients with type 2 diabetes (p=0.04). This suggests that the association between TCF7L2 polymorphisms and gastric cancer may be different in patients with type 2 diabetes, and further studies are needed to investigate this potential interaction.

In conclusion, our study suggests that the rs7903146 polymorphism in the TCF7L2 gene is not associated with gastric cancer in the Venezuelan population as a whole, but may be associated with gastric cancer in patients with type 2 diabetes. Further studies are needed to confirm these findings and to investigate the mechanisms underlying the association between TCF7L2 polymorphisms and gastric cancer.

question: Does maternal pravastatin prevent altered fetal brain development in a preeclamptic CD-1 mouse model?
ground_truth: Preeclampsia alters brain development in sex-specific patterns, and prenatal pravastatin therapy prevents altered neuroanatomic programming in this animal model.
baseline_prediction: Preeclampsia is a pregnancy complication characterized by hypertension, proteinuria, and placental ischemia. Although the cause is unknown, it is hypothesized that chronic oxidative stress plays a role in its pathogenesis. The aim of this study was to investigate the effects of maternal pravastatin on placental ischemia and altered fetal brain development in a preeclamptic CD-1 mouse model. The study was conducted by exposing pregnant mice to pravastatin (10 mg/kg) or vehicle daily from gestational day (GD) 9.5 to GD 19.5. On GD 19.5, a uterine artery embolization procedure was performed to induce placental ischemia, and the pups were sacrificed on postnatal day 21. Preeclampsia was induced in the vehicle group and pravastatin-treated mice had reduced placental ischemia and improved brain development. Pravastatin treatment resulted in decreased oxidative stress in the placenta and brain, and increased anti-oxidant enzymes in the placenta. In conclusion, maternal pravastatin reduced placental ischemia and improved brain development in a preeclamptic CD-1 mouse model.

question: Is revised Cardiac Risk Index ( RCRI ) a useful tool for evaluation of perioperative cardiac morbidity in kidney transplant recipients?
ground_truth: The RCRI is a useful tool for cardiac risk stratification in kidney transplantation and could be used to develop protocols for intraoperative and postoperative care to minimize complications.
baseline_prediction:
The revised cardiac risk index (RCRI) is a useful tool for evaluation of perioperative cardiac morbidity in kidney transplant recipients.

Explanation: Cardiac risk assessment for non-cardiac surgery is well established. The revised cardiac risk index (RCRI) is a validated risk stratification tool used for non-cardiac surgery. The aim of the present study was to determine whether the RCRI is a useful tool for evaluation of perioperative cardiac morbidity in kidney transplant recipients. Methods: We retrospectively reviewed data from 106 kidney transplant recipients who underwent non-cardiac surgery between 2009 and 2013. Perioperative cardiac events were defined as cardiac ischemia, arrhythmias, and myocardial infarction. Results: The mean age of patients was 54.2 years, and 58.5% were male. The mean follow-up period was 17.8 months. The incidence of perioperative cardiac morbidity was 24.5% (26 patients). The patients with cardiac events were older (65.7 ± 7.1 years vs. 52.1 ± 9.7 years, P = 0.001), had a higher incidence of hypertension (89.6% vs. 59.7%, P = 0.005), a higher number of comorbidities (3.1 ± 1.3 vs. 2.4 ± 1.3, P = 0.004), and a higher number of RCRI (1.5 ± 0.8 vs. 0.5 ± 0.6, P = 0.001) than those without cardiac events. Multivariate logistic regression analysis showed that the number of RCRI (P = 0.008) and the number of comorbidities (P = 0.042) were independent risk factors for cardiac events. Conclusion: The RCRI is a useful tool for evaluation of perioperative cardiac morbidity in kidney transplant recipients.

question: Are bacterial biofilms on implanted suture material a cause of surgical site infection?
ground_truth: Bacterial biofilms on implanted suture material can manifest as persistent surgical site infections that require complete removal of the underlying foreign body substrata for resolution.
baseline_prediction:
Although not a direct cause, biofilms are known to create a more favorable environment for bacterial growth. Biofilms may contribute to chronic inflammation, which can lead to the development of chronic infections such as osteomyelitis. Additionally, biofilms can make it difficult to completely eradicate bacteria, leading to the persistence of infection. Finally, biofilms can protect bacteria from the host immune system, making it more difficult to clear the infection. Overall, biofilms can contribute to the development and persistence of surgical site infections, but they are not the sole cause.

Explanation: Bacterial biofilms are a common cause of chronic infections, including surgical site infections. Biofilms are defined as surface-attached communities of microorganisms that are surrounded by a matrix of extracellular polymers. These polymers provide a protective environment for the bacteria, allowing them to survive and thrive in harsh environments. Biofilms are thought to contribute to the development and persistence of surgical site infections by several mechanisms. First, biofilms can protect bacteria from the host immune system, making it more difficult to clear the infection. Second, biofilms can make it difficult to completely eradicate bacteria, leading to the persistence of infection. Finally, biofilms can create a more favorable environment for bacterial growth, leading to chronic inflammation and the development of chronic infections such as osteomyelitis. In conclusion, bacterial biofilms are a common cause of surgical site infections and can contribute to the development and persistence of these infections.

question: Do fGF2 gene activated matrices promote proliferation of bone marrow stromal cells?
ground_truth: This first in vitro evaluation in BMSCs provides evidence that gene activated matrices (GAMs) encoding the FGF2 protein may have strong translational potential for clinical applications that require enhanced osseous and periodontal tissue regeneration.
baseline_prediction:
FGF2 gene activated matrices promote proliferation of bone marrow stromal cells.

Explanation: Fibroblast growth factor (FGF) is a protein involved in development, wound repair, and angiogenesis. Fibroblast growth factor-2 (FGF2) is a protein that stimulates the proliferation of cells, particularly in the form of FGF2 gene activated matrices (FAMs). FAMs are matrices of collagen type I, FGF2, and basic fibroblast growth factor (bFGF) that induce the proliferation of bone marrow stromal cells (BMSCs). The present study aimed to assess the proliferation of BMSCs in response to FAMs. BMSCs were cultured in a 3D culture system on FAMs in the presence of FGF2. The cells were then stained for alpha-smooth muscle actin and collagen type I and quantified using flow cytometry. The results showed that the expression of alpha-smooth muscle actin and collagen type I increased in the BMSCs cultured on FAMs in the presence of FGF2. This study demonstrated that FAMs promote the proliferation of BMSCs in response to FGF2, which may have implications for tissue engineering and regenerative medicine.

question: Is low-frequency neuromuscular depression a consequence of a reduction in nerve terminal Ca2+ currents at mammalian motor nerve endings?
ground_truth: The results demonstrate that neuromuscular depression during train-of-four monitoring is due to a decline in nerve terminal Ca currents, hence reducing the release of acetylcholine. As similar processes may come into play at higher stimulation frequencies, agents that antagonize the decline in Ca currents could be used to treat conditions in which neuromuscular depression can be debilitating.
baseline_prediction:
Low-frequency neuromuscular depression is a consequence of a reduction in nerve terminal Ca2+ currents at mammalian motor nerve endings. Low-frequency stimulation of mammalian motor nerve endings leads to a reduction in the number of Ca2+ channels available for opening at the end plate, thus reducing the Ca2+ influx and the amount of acetylcholine released. This in turn leads to a decrease in the number of postsynaptic receptors activated, resulting in reduced muscle contraction. This process is known as low-frequency neuromuscular depression.

The key concept here is that low-frequency stimulation leads to a reduction in the number of Ca2+ channels available for opening, which reduces the amount of Ca2+ influx and the amount of acetylcholine released. This leads to a decrease in the number of postsynaptic receptors activated, resulting in reduced muscle contraction.

This concept can be further explored by looking at the mechanisms underlying the reduction in Ca2+ channels available for opening. It has been shown that low-frequency stimulation leads to the activation of Ca2+-dependent K+ channels, which increases the K+ influx and hyperpolarizes the membrane potential. This hyperpolarization reduces the likelihood of Ca2+ channels opening, thus reducing the amount of Ca2+ influx and the amount of acetylcholine released. This in turn leads to a decrease in the number of postsynaptic receptors activated, resulting in reduced muscle contraction.

In conclusion, low-frequency neuromuscular depression is a consequence of a reduction in nerve terminal Ca2+ currents at mammalian motor nerve endings. Low-frequency stimulation leads to a reduction in the number of Ca2+ channels available for opening, which reduces the amount of Ca2+ influx and the amount of acetylcholine released. This leads to a decrease in the number of postsynaptic receptors activated, resulting in reduced muscle contraction. The mechanisms underlying this process include the activation of Ca2+-dependent K+ channels, which increase the K+ influx and hyperpolarize the membrane potential, thus reducing the likelihood of Ca2+ channels opening.

The hypothesis that decreases in nerve terminal Ca2+ currents are responsible for low-frequency depression has been evaluated using simultaneous electrophysiological measurements of neurotransmitter release and prejunctional Ca currents at mouse neuromuscular junctions.

The results showed that low-frequency depression of transmitter release at mouse neuromuscular junctions is not due to changes in nerve terminal Ca2+ currents. Instead, low-frequency depression is mediated by a reduction in presynaptic Ca2+ influx through calcium release channels.

Overall, these findings suggest that low-frequency depression is a complex process involving multiple mechanisms and may have implications for understanding the mechanism of neuromuscular depression in clinical settings.

In conclusion, low-frequency depression of transmitter release at mouse neuromuscular junctions is not due to changes in nerve terminal Ca2+ currents, but rather is mediated by a reduction in presynaptic Ca2+ influx through calcium release channels. These findings have implications for understanding the mechanism of neuromuscular depression in clinical settings.

Answer: Low-frequency neuromuscular depression is a phenomenon where the strength of muscle contraction is reduced in response to a low-frequency electrical stimulation. This phenomenon is widely used in clinical settings to assess the type and degree of neuromuscular block.

ground_truth: Cordycepin plus cisplatin have better apoptotic effect by activating caspase activation with possible MAPK pathway involvement in HNSCC cells.

Cordycepin is a metabolite of Cordyceps militaris that has been shown to have antitumor effects. This study investigated whether cordycepin could enhance the apoptotic effect of cisplatin in human head and neck tumor cells by targeting the caspase/MAPK signaling pathway. The results showed that cordycepin could significantly enhance the cytotoxicity of cisplatin in human head and neck tumor cells. The mechanism by which cordycepin enhances the apoptotic effect of cisplatin was found to be mediated by the caspase/MAPK signaling pathway. Specifically, cordycepin upregulated the expression of caspase-3 and downregulated the expression of MAPK, resulting in the activation of caspase-3 and the induction of apoptosis. This study provides a new insight into the potential of cordycepin as an adjuvant therapy for head and neck cancer and suggests that it may be a promising candidate for further investigation.

question: Is elevated factor IX activity associated with an increased odds ratio for both arterial and venous thrombotic events?
ground_truth: Our findings suggest an association between elevated FIX levels and both arterial and venous thrombotic events.
baseline_prediction:
The question asks to determine whether elevated factor IX activity is associated with an increased risk of thrombotic events. The question could be answered by using a retrospective analysis of data from a large cohort study or a prospective study with regular follow-up. A prospective study would be preferred over a retrospective study because it would provide a better estimate of the association between elevated factor IX activity and thrombotic events. A prospective study would also allow for the identification of potential confounding factors that could affect the association between elevated factor IX activity and thrombotic events.

The following is a possible response to the question:

The study design used for this analysis was a prospective, population-based cohort study. The study included a large number of participants who were followed for an average of 10 years. The primary outcome of interest was the occurrence of thrombotic events (both arterial and venous). The exposure of interest was elevated factor IX activity, which was measured in a subset of participants.

The analysis showed that participants with elevated factor IX activity had a significantly increased risk of thrombotic events. The odds ratio for thrombotic events was 2.4 (95% confidence interval: 1.9-3.1). This finding remained statistically significant even after adjusting for potential confounding factors such as age, sex, smoking status, and hypertension.

The study findings suggest that elevated factor IX activity is associated with an increased risk of thrombotic events. This finding has important implications for the management of patients with thrombotic disorders.

question: Do endothelial microparticles correlate with high-risk angiographic lesions in acute coronary syndromes?
ground_truth: High EMP was associated with high-risk angiographic lesions including eccentric type II, multiple irregular, and lesions with thrombi. Mild to moderate stenosis was associated with higher EMP levels than severe stenosis. EMP may be a useful marker in detecting endothelial injury and risk of ACS as defined by angiography.
baseline_prediction:
**The presence of endothelial microparticles correlates with high-risk angiographic lesions in acute coronary syndromes.** Endothelial microparticles are released from the endothelium in response to vascular injury. They are associated with the development of atherosclerosis and thrombosis. Endothelial microparticles in the circulation are increased in patients with acute coronary syndromes and are associated with a worse prognosis. The study by Lecour S et al. (2011) assessed the association between endothelial microparticles and angiographic lesion characteristics in patients with acute coronary syndromes. The study included 106 patients who underwent angiography within 24 hours of presentation with acute coronary syndromes. Endothelial microparticles were measured by flow cytometry. Angiographic lesion characteristics were assessed by quantitative coronary angiography. The study found that the presence of endothelial microparticles was associated with a higher prevalence of complex lesions, including thrombus and large-vessel disease. These findings suggest that endothelial microparticles may be a marker of a more advanced and complex form of atherosclerosis in patients with acute coronary syndromes.

This study has several strengths. First, it was a prospective study, which reduces the risk of selection bias. Second, the study used objective measures of endothelial microparticle levels and angiographic lesion characteristics. Third, the study included a large number of patients, which improves the generalizability of the findings. Finally, the study was conducted by a team of experienced researchers.

This study also has some limitations. First, the study was performed in a single center, which limits the generalizability of the findings. Second, the study did not assess the clinical outcomes of the patients, which limits our ability to draw conclusions about the prognostic significance of endothelial microparticles. Finally, the study did not assess the underlying cause of the endothelial microparticle release, which limits our ability to make causal inferences.

In conclusion, this study found that the presence of endothelial microparticles correlates with high-risk angiographic lesions in acute coronary syndromes. Endothelial microparticles may be a marker of a more advanced and complex form of atherosclerosis in these patients. However, further research is needed to confirm these findings and to better understand the role of endothelial microparticles in the pathophysiology of acute coronary syndromes.

question: Does dietary carbohydrate modification enhance insulin secretion in persons with the metabolic syndrome?
ground_truth: Rye bread and pasta-based carbohydrate modification enhances early insulin secretion in persons with the metabolic syndrome, which may lower the risk of deteriorating glucose tolerance and development of type 2 diabetes.
baseline_prediction:
Dietary carbohydrate modification enhances insulin secretion in persons with the metabolic syndrome

A 2016 review by Bazzano and colleagues found evidence that dietary carbohydrate modification might enhance insulin secretion in persons with the metabolic syndrome. The authors concluded that reducing dietary carbohydrates by 25% to 50% and replacing it with protein and fat might be beneficial in persons with the metabolic syndrome. The authors stated that further studies are needed to confirm this finding.

question: Do rBCs labeled at two biotin densities permit simultaneous and repeated measurements of circulating RBC volume?
ground_truth: Using RBCs labeled with two different densities of biotin, RBC volume can be accurately measured simultaneously and repeatedly in the same subject without radiation exposure.
baseline_prediction: Yes. This is the principle of two-color flow cytometry.
rag_prediction:
No. There are no two populations of RBCs labeled with low-density biotin (LDB) and high-density biotin (HDB).

Explanation:
The context provides information about the extend potential applications of a nonradioactive method for measuring circulating red blood cell (RBC) volume. It states that RBCs labeled with LDB and HDB can be mixed and transfused. However, it does not provide any information about the existence of two populations of RBCs labeled with different biotin densities. Thus, the question "Do rBCs labeled at two biotin densities permit simultaneous and repeated measurements of circulating RBC volume?" is based on an assumption that is not present in the context. Therefore, the answer should be "No. There are no two populations of RBCs labeled with low-density biotin (LDB) and high-density biotin (HDB)."

question: Do autotoxic ginsenosides in the rhizosphere contribute to the replant failure of Panax notoginseng?
ground_truth: Our results demonstrated that autotoxicity results in replant failure of Sanqi ginseng. While Sanqi ginseng consecutively cultivated, some ginsenosides can accumulate in rhizosphere soils through root exudates or root decomposition, which impedes seedling emergence and growth.
baseline_prediction:
The replant failure of Panax notoginseng, a medicinal plant widely used in traditional Chinese medicine, is a complex phenomenon that has been studied extensively over the years. One hypothesis suggests that autotoxic ginsenosides, a class of ginsenosides that are toxic to the plant itself, may contribute to the replant failure. These ginsenosides are produced by the plant itself and can accumulate in the soil around the plant's roots. This accumulation can lead to the death of the plant, known as replant failure. The hypothesis states that the presence of autotoxic ginsenosides in the soil can interfere with the plant's ability to uptake nutrients and water, leading to its death. This hypothesis has been supported by experimental studies that have shown that the presence of autotoxic ginsenosides can indeed lead to replant failure in Panax notoginseng. However, further research is needed to fully understand the role of autotoxic ginsenosides in replant failure, and to determine whether other factors, such as soil pH or nutrient availability, may also contribute to this phenomenon.

question: Does perinatal supplementation with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids improve sevoflurane-induced neurodegeneration and memory impairment in neonatal rats?
ground_truth: Our study demonstrated that neonatal exposure to prolonged sevoflurane could impair the immediate fear response, working memory and short-term memory of rats at their adulthood, which may through inducing neuronal apoptosis and decreasing neurogenesis. However, these sevoflurane-induced unfavorable neuronal effects can be mitigated by perinatal n-3 PUFAs supplementation.
baseline_prediction:
No, it did not.

Explanation: A prospective, randomized, double-blinded, placebo-controlled clinical trial was conducted to investigate the effect of oral supplementation with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids on sevoflurane-induced neurodegeneration and memory impairment in neonatal rats. The study was performed on 60 neonatal rats divided into 6 groups (10 rats per group) as follows: group 1: control rats, group 2: rats with sevoflurane exposure, group 3: rats with sevoflurane exposure and oral supplementation with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, group 4: rats with sevoflurane exposure and oral supplementation with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and amiodarone, group 5: rats with sevoflurane exposure and oral supplementation with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and propofol, and group 6: rats with sevoflurane exposure and oral supplementation with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lidocaine. Rats were exposed to sevoflurane for 8 h. The rats were then sacrificed and their brains were removed and processed for histopathological examination. In addition, the rats were evaluated for memory impairment using the Morris water maze test. The results showed that exposure to sevoflurane caused neurodegeneration and memory impairment. Oral supplementation with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids alone did not improve neurodegeneration and memory impairment. Oral supplementation with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids in combination with amiodarone, propofol, or lidocaine did not improve neurodegeneration and memory impairment. The results of this study suggest that oral supplementation with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids alone does not improve sevoflurane-induced neurodegeneration and memory impairment in neonatal rats. However, oral supplementation with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids in combination with amiodarone, propofol, or lidocaine may improve sevoflurane-induced neurodegeneration and memory impairment in neonatal rats.

question: Do human breast cancer associated fibroblasts exhibit subtype specific gene expression profiles?
ground_truth: These data demonstrate that in addition to the distinct molecular profiles that characterize the neoplastic cells, CAF gene expression is also differentially regulated in distinct subtypes of breast cancer.
baseline_prediction:
Yes, breast cancer associated fibroblasts exhibit subtype specific gene expression profiles.

Answer: 1. Yes, breast cancer associated fibroblasts exhibit subtype specific gene expression profiles.

2. No, breast cancer associated fibroblasts do not exhibit subtype specific gene expression profiles.

3. Maybe, breast cancer associated fibroblasts exhibit subtype specific gene expression profiles in some cases but not in others.

4. Breast cancer associated fibroblasts exhibit subtype specific gene expression profiles only if they are cultured in a particular type of medium.
rag_prediction:
Yes, human breast cancer associated fibroblasts exhibit subtype specific gene expression profiles.

Explanation: Breast cancer is a heterogeneous disease for which prognosis and treatment strategies are largely governed by the receptor status (estrogen, progesterone and Her2) of the tumor cells. Gene expression profiling of whole breast tumors further stratifies breast cancer into several molecular subtypes which also co-segregate with the receptor status of the tumor cells. We postulated that cancer associated fibroblasts (CAFs) within the tumor stroma may exhibit subtype specific gene expression profiles. To test this hypothesis, we performed gene expression profiling on fibroblasts obtained from primary breast tumors. We found that the gene expression profiles of CAFs from normal breast tissue are distinct from those of CAFs from breast cancer, and that the gene expression profiles of CAFs from tumors are also distinct from each other. Interestingly, the gene expression profiles of CAFs from different breast cancer subtypes were more similar to each other than to CAFs from normal breast tissue. We therefore conclude that CAFs within breast tumors have a gene expression profile that is associated with the subtype of the primary breast cancer from which they are derived, and that the gene expression profile of CAFs within normal breast tissue is distinct from that of CAFs within breast cancer. question: Does cryopreservation time decrease follicular viability in ovarian tissue frozen for fertility preservation?
ground_truth: The slow freezing procedures used for ovarian cryopreservation are capable of preserving follicular viability and maintaining the steroidogenic capacity of the tissue despite a roughly 30% decrease in follicular viability. Furthermore, short-term storage of ovarian tissue does not appear to compromise follicle integrity.
baseline_prediction: Cryopreservation of ovarian tissue has been shown to be an effective method for fertility preservation in patients at risk of ovarian failure. However, it is still unclear whether the duration of cryopreservation has an impact on the viability of follicles. In this study, we aimed to investigate the impact of cryopreservation time on the viability of follicles in cryopreserved ovarian tissue. We collected data from 15 patients who underwent fertility preservation surgery for cancer treatment. Ovarian tissue was frozen using the vitrification method and cryopreservation time was recorded. Follicular density was quantified using the counting method, and follicular viability was assessed using the vital dye calcein AM. The results showed that follicular density decreased with increasing cryopreservation time. In addition, follicular viability decreased with increasing cryopreservation time. These findings suggest that cryopreservation time has a significant impact on follicular viability in cryopreserved ovarian tissue. Further research is needed to investigate the optimal duration of cryopreservation for follicular viability preservation.
rag_prediction:
The provided context indicates that follicular viability decreases with increasing cryopreservation time, as indicated by the decrease in follicular density and follicular viability. This suggests that cryopreservation for longer durations may lead to a decrease in follicular viability.

Explanation: Cryopreservation involves the process of freezing cells or tissues to preserve them for future use. This technique is commonly used in fertility preservation for women undergoing cancer treatment or those who need to delay childbearing due to medical reasons. However, prolonged cryopreservation can lead to decreased viability of the cells or tissues, resulting in reduced fertility potential.

The context provided suggests that follicular viability decreases with increasing cryopreservation time. Follicular density, a measure of the number of follicles present, is significantly lower in ovarian tissue that has been cryopreserved for longer durations compared to fresh samples. Additionally, follicular viability, a measure of the ability of the follicles to undergo maturation and ovulation, is also found to decrease with increasing cryopreservation time. These findings indicate that follicular viability is negatively impacted by prolonged cryopreservation, suggesting that cryopreservation for longer durations may lead to decreased fertility potential.

Overall, the provided context suggests that cryopreservation time plays a significant role in determining the viability of follicles in ovarian tissue. Longer cryopreservation times can result in reduced fertility potential due to decreased viability of the follicles. Therefore, it is crucial to consider the duration of cryopreservation when preserving ovarian tissue for fertility purposes.

question: Is the Wnt signalling pathway upregulated in an in vitro model of acquired tamoxifen resistant breast cancer?
ground_truth: These data support the role of the Wnt signalling pathway in acquired resistance to Tamoxifen. Further research into the mechanism by which activated Wnt signalling inhibits the effects of Tamoxifen should be undertaken. As a number of small molecules targeting the Wnt pathway are currently in pre-clinical development, combinatorial treatment with endocrine agents and Wnt pathway inhibitors may be a useful therapeutic option in the future for a subset of breast cancer patients.
baseline_prediction:
The Wnt signalling pathway is upregulated in an in vitro model of acquired tamoxifen resistant breast cancer. This was assessed using qRT-PCR to quantify mRNA expression of the Wnt pathway target genes Axin2 and Lef1.

**Explanation of answer**

The Wnt signalling pathway is a major signalling pathway that regulates many important cellular processes including cell fate, proliferation, survival, migration and stem cell maintenance. In breast cancer, Wnt signalling is upregulated in many cancer types, and is linked to poor prognosis and increased resistance to chemotherapy. In particular, Wnt signalling is upregulated in acquired tamoxifen resistant breast cancer, which is a form of endocrine resistant breast cancer that occurs in around 20% of patients who initially respond to tamoxifen treatment. Acquired tamoxifen resistance is associated with increased proliferation and metastasis, as well as resistance to other endocrine therapies such as aromatase inhibitors. Therefore, it is important to identify the molecular mechanisms underlying acquired tamoxifen resistance in order to develop more effective treatments.

To investigate the role of the Wnt signalling pathway in acquired tamoxifen resistant breast cancer, an in vitro model of acquired tamoxifen resistance was created using the breast cancer cell line MCF-7. MCF-7 cells were treated with increasing concentrations of tamoxifen for 2 weeks, and the cells that were resistant to tamoxifen were selected and grown in the presence of 100 nM tamoxifen. The cells were then analysed for changes in Wnt signalling pathway gene expression using qRT-PCR.

The results showed that the expression of the Wnt pathway target genes Axin2 and Lef1 was significantly increased in the tamoxifen resistant cells compared to the parental MCF-7 cells. This suggests that the Wnt signalling pathway is upregulated in an in vitro model of acquired tamoxifen resistant breast cancer, which is consistent with previous studies showing increased Wnt signalling in acquired tamoxifen resistant breast cancer in vivo. These findings provide evidence that the Wnt signalling pathway plays a key role in acquired tamoxifen resistance and may be a potential target for therapy.

question: Do readmission rates after lower extremity bypass vary significantly by surgical indication?
ground_truth: The 30-day readmission rates after lower extremity bypass vary significantly by surgical indication. Because lower extremity bypasses are performed for multiple indications, if readmission rates are publically reported and hospitals can be penalized for higher than expected readmission rates, the expected readmission rates should be adjusted for surgical indication.
baseline_prediction:
No. The rate of readmission after lower extremity bypass was 22.0% and did not vary significantly by surgical indication. 

Explanation: 

Lower extremity bypass is an effective treatment for limb salvage in patients with peripheral arterial disease. We sought to determine whether readmission rates after lower extremity bypass vary significantly by surgical indication.

We reviewed the inpatient discharge records of all patients undergoing lower extremity bypass at our institution from January 1, 2008, through December 31, 2011. The primary outcome was readmission within 30 days of discharge. Univariate and multivariate logistic regression analyses were performed to evaluate the association between readmission and patient and procedural characteristics. 

We identified 1,013 patients undergoing lower extremity bypass. The overall 30-day readmission rate was 22.0%. Among 1,005 patients with a discharge diagnosis, the rate of readmission was 22.0%. Among 1,013 patients with a discharge diagnosis, the rate of readmission was 21.6% (95% confidence interval [CI], 18.1%-25.3%). The rate of readmission was not significantly different by surgical indication, with the following rates: 21.5% (95% CI, 17.8%-25.5%) for atherosclerosis; 22.6% (95% CI, 17.4%-28.7%) for chronic venous insufficiency; 18.2% (95% CI, 12.2%-25.3%) for trauma; 21.8% (95% CI, 16.2%-28.3%) for aneurysm; and 22.7% (95% CI, 17.1%-29.2%) for other indications. Multivariate logistic regression demonstrated that older age, hypertension, congestive heart failure, diabetes, and renal failure were associated with increased odds of readmission. 

The rate of readmission after lower extremity bypass was 22.0% and did not vary significantly by surgical indication.

question: Does collagen antibody-induced arthritis evoke persistent pain with spinal glial involvement and transient prostaglandin dependency?
ground_truth: CAIA represents a multifaceted model for studies exploring the mechanisms of pain induced by inflammation in the articular joint. Our findings of a time-dependent prostaglandin and spinal glial contribution to antibody-induced pain highlight the importance of using appropriate disease models to assess joint-related pain.
baseline_prediction:
The collagen antibody-induced arthritis (CAIA) model of chronic pain and glial activation is a useful model to study the contribution of glia to pain, and may provide an opportunity to develop novel pain therapies.

Answer: Collagen antibody-induced arthritis (CAIA) is a model of chronic pain in which a chronic inflammatory process involving glial cells and activated microglia is induced by repeated injections of collagen antibodies into the knee joints of mice.

The CAIA model has been extensively studied in the past decade and has become a valuable tool for understanding the mechanisms underlying chronic pain and the role of glia in the development of pain. The model has been used to investigate the contribution of glial cells to pain, and to test potential treatments for pain.

One of the strengths of the CAIA model is that it can be used to study the contribution of individual glial cell types to pain. For example, studies have shown that microglia are involved in the development of chronic pain in the CAIA model, and that blocking the activity of microglia can reduce pain behavior. This is important because microglia are known to be involved in the development of chronic pain in other models, such as the spinal nerve ligation model.

The CAIA model has also been used to study the role of other glial cell types, such as astrocytes and oligodendrocytes. For example, studies have shown that astrocytes are involved in the development of chronic pain in the CAIA model, and that blocking the activity of astrocytes can reduce pain behavior.

The CAIA model has also been used to study the role of glial cells in the development of pain tolerance. Studies have shown that glial cells are involved in the development of pain tolerance in the CAIA model, and that blocking the activity of glial cells can reduce pain tolerance.

The CAIA model has also been used to study the role of glial cells in the development of neuropathic pain. Studies have shown that glial cells are involved in the development of neuropathic pain in the CAIA model, and that blocking the activity of glial cells can reduce neuropathic pain behavior.

Overall, the CAIA model is a valuable tool for studying the contribution of glia to pain and the development of novel treatments for pain.

question: Is a 68-kD antigen , which probably an N-terminal fragment of the VLA-5 alpha 5-subunit , is specific for differentiating keratinocytes?
ground_truth: The monoclonal antibody 8D9 reacts with the alpha 5-subunit of human VLA-5 integrin and with a 68-kD antigen that is strongly expressed in differentiating keratinocytes in vitro and in the cornified layers of human skin in vivo. Psoriatic skin showed additional immunoreactivity in the upper spinous and granular layers. Based on indirect immunological and chemical evidence we suggest that the 68-kD protein is an amino-terminal degradation product of the alpha 5-subunit, which provides a new and interesting marker of differentiating keratinocytes.
baseline_prediction: The question is about antigenic differences in keratinocytes. The question is not about the antigenic difference in keratinocytes and differentiating keratinocytes. The answer to the question is "yes", a 68-kD antigen is an N-terminal fragment of the VLA-5 alpha subunit.

question: Do oxygen therapy for severe corneal alkali burn in rabbits?
ground_truth: Oxygen therapy at a flow of 5 L/min for 1 hour daily reduces the possibility of corneal perforation in rabbits and may delay ulceration of the cornea compared with the control group.
baseline_prediction: Oxygen therapy has been shown to be effective in treating severe corneal alkali burns in rabbits. Oxygen therapy was found to be effective in reducing the extent of corneal damage and promoting healing. The use of oxygen therapy in combination with other treatments such as antibiotics and steroids was also found to be more effective in treating severe corneal alkali burns. In conclusion, oxygen therapy is an effective treatment for severe corneal alkali burns in rabbits.

question: Does h-NST induce LEE expression and the formation of attaching and effacing lesions in enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli?
ground_truth: H-NST induces LEE expression and A/E lesion formation likely by counteracting H-NS-mediated repression. We demonstrate that H-NST binds to DNA and identify arginine residues that are functionally important for DNA-binding. Our study suggests that H-NST provides an additional means for A/E pathogens to alleviate repression of virulence gene expression by H-NS to promote virulence capabilities.
baseline_prediction: Attaching and effacing (A/E) lesions are characteristic of enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli (EHEC) and other enteropathogenic bacteria. The A/E lesion is a site of intimate interaction between the pathogen and the host intestinal epithelium. This lesion is characterized by the presence of a pedestal-like projection formed by the actin-rich tail of the pathogen, as well as the presence of the translocated intimin receptor (Tir) of the host cell. The E. coli pathogenicity island (PAI) is a genomic region that is associated with the production of a type III secretion system (TTSS) that is responsible for the secretion of effector proteins into the host cell cytoplasm. The TTSS is a complex of more than 25 proteins that is responsible for the translocation of effector proteins. The PAI of EHEC is responsible for the production of the LEE (locus of enterocyte effacement), which encodes the proteins of the TTSS. Tir is an important receptor of the TTSS and is expressed exclusively in enterocytes. EHEC is a common cause of hemorrhagic colitis in humans. The pathogenesis of EHEC is associated with the formation of A/E lesions. The formation of A/E lesions is dependent on the expression of the LEE-encoded proteins. The expression of the LEE-encoded proteins is dependent on the h-NST gene. h-NST is a member of the PAI. h-NST is an important regulator of the LEE-encoded proteins. The expression of h-NST is regulated by the hns gene, which is a global regulator of the PAI. The hns gene is regulated by the EHEC-encoded H-NS protein. The H-NS protein is a global regulator of the EHEC genome. H-NS is a member of the family of global regulators of the PAI. H-NS regulates the expression of h-NST and, consequently, the expression of the LEE-encoded proteins and the formation of A/E lesions. The H-NS protein is a member of the family of global regulators of the PAI. H-NS regulates the expression of h-NST and, consequently, the expression of the LEE-encoded proteins and the formation of A/E lesions. question: Do aDP-degrading enzymes inhibit platelet activation in bile duct-ligated rats?
ground_truth: Bile duct ligation does not affect intrinsic platelet function, but impairs platelet activation via release of ADP-degrading enzymes in the circulation.
baseline_prediction: Inhibition of platelet activation is a key step in the pathogenesis of bile duct-ligated rats. ADP-degrading enzymes have been identified as potential inhibitors of platelet activation in this model. Further research is needed to determine the efficacy and safety of ADP-degrading enzymes in this context.
rag_prediction: Yes, aDP-degrading enzymes inhibit platelet activation in bile duct-ligated rats. The effect of cholestatic liver disease on primary hemostasis function remains ill-defined. To determine platelet function and identify the mechanisms involved in the observed platelet function in cholestatic rats, platelet function was studied in a model of 2-week bile duct ligation and compared to that in sham-operated rats with and without a storage pool defect. ADP-induced and collagen-induced platelet aggregation were clearly impaired following bile duct ligation (P<0.05). Interestingly, a significant increase in the plasma level of aDP-degrading enzymes, which was observed after bile duct ligation, was associated with a decrease in platelet aggregation. These data suggest that the impaired platelet function observed in cholestatic rats is at least partially due to an increase in aDP-degrading enzymes.

question: Does the dipeptidyl peptidase-4 inhibitor vildagliptin affect ex vivo cytokine response and lymphocyte function in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus?
ground_truth: These data show that a four-week treatment with vildagliptin in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus does not result in a significant modulation of cytokine responses. This observation suggests that inhibition of DDP-4 does not lead to an increased risk of infection by diminishing cytokine production.
baseline_prediction:
Dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) inhibitors are a new class of antidiabetic drugs that are increasingly used in the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus. This study aimed to evaluate the effect of vildagliptin on ex vivo cytokine response and lymphocyte function in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Twenty patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus were enrolled in this study. Patients were randomly assigned to a vildagliptin treatment group (10 patients) or a placebo group (10 patients). Patients were treated with vildagliptin 50 mg once daily for 8 weeks, or placebo for 8 weeks. Before and after treatment, ex vivo cytokine response and lymphocyte function were evaluated using a flow cytometry assay.

After treatment, there was no significant difference in ex vivo cytokine response or lymphocyte function between the two groups. However, there was a significant difference in the proportion of CD4+ T cells and the ratio of CD4+/CD8+ T cells between the two groups. The proportion of CD4+ T cells and the ratio of CD4+/CD8+ T cells were significantly higher in the vildagliptin group than in the placebo group.

Vildagliptin treatment may have a beneficial effect on ex vivo cytokine response and lymphocyte function in patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus. However, further studies are needed to confirm these findings.

question: Does prevalence and correlate of depressive symptoms in adult patients with pulmonary tuberculosis in the Southwest Region of Cameroon?
ground_truth: Our study suggests that there is a high prevalence of depression among PTB patients, with more than one in two patients affected. Multidisciplinary care for TB patients involving mental health practitioners is highly encouraged, especially for high-risk groups.
baseline_prediction:
The prevalence of depressive symptoms among adult patients with pulmonary tuberculosis was 34.3%, with a higher prevalence among women (40.0%) compared to men (25.7%). The prevalence of depressive symptoms was higher among patients with pulmonary TB of > 2 months' duration (37.0%), current smokers (42.9%), and those with a history of alcohol abuse (47.4%).

Reference: [1](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0187596)

Explanation: This is a cross-sectional study done in the Southwest region of Cameroon. 130 adult patients with pulmonary tuberculosis were recruited and interviewed using the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9). Depressive symptoms were diagnosed if PHQ-9 score was > or =10. Socio-demographic and clinical data were also collected.

The author concluded that the prevalence of depressive symptoms among adult patients with pulmonary tuberculosis in the Southwest Region of Cameroon was 34.3%. They also noted that women, patients with pulmonary TB of > 2 months' duration, current smokers, and those with a history of alcohol abuse had a higher prevalence of depressive symptoms.

What is the significance of this article?

This article is significant because it highlights the high prevalence of depressive symptoms among adult patients with pulmonary tuberculosis in the Southwest Region of Cameroon. This emphasizes the need for clinicians to screen for and treat depressive symptoms in this population, as it can affect the patient's quality of life and prognosis. Additionally, the article provides valuable insights into the sociodemographic and clinical factors associated with depressive symptoms, which can inform future research and clinical practice.

question: Does rET proto-oncogene testing in infants presenting with Hirschsprung disease identify 2 new multiple endocrine neoplasia 2A kindreds?
ground_truth: Hirschsprung disease can be the initial presentation of MEN 2A. We strongly recommend that genetic screening be performed in patients presenting with HSCR, looking for the known RET mutations associated with MEN 2A. If a mutation consistent with MEN 2A is detected, genetic screening of all first-degree relatives in the kindred is recommended.
baseline_prediction: The answer to the question posed by the article is 'Yes'. The study reports on the identification of two new kindreds with multiple endocrine neoplasia 2A, based on the presence of RET proto-oncogene mutations in two affected infants with Hirschsprung disease. The identification of such mutations in patients presenting with Hirschsprung disease is of interest in two ways. Firstly, the presence of RET proto-oncogene mutations in infants with Hirschsprung disease does not appear to be a marker for multiple endocrine neoplasia 2A. Secondly, the authors point out that the discovery of these mutations may be of interest to families with multiple endocrine neoplasia 2A who have not been identified to date.

question: Is loss of CD151/Tspan24 from the complex with integrin α3β1 in invasive front of the tumour a negative predictor of disease-free survival in oral squamous cell carcinoma?
ground_truth: There was no statistical association between any of the proteins scored in W and clinicopathologic features or patient survival. When examined separately, significant associations were shown for (i) CD151 and EGFR in TU (p=0.036) and (ii) tumour grade and EGFR in both TU (p=0.045) and IF (p=0.030). INGA3 was present predominantly in the tumour-host interface, significantly stronger in IF than TU (p=0.021). An association with 5-year disease-free survival was close to significant for INGA3 (TU and IF) (p=0.050) but not the CD151/INGA3 complex. Expression of CD151/INGA3 at the IF might reflect tumour behaviour pertinent to patient outcome.
baseline_prediction:
The loss of CD151/Tspan24 from the complex with integrin α3β1 in invasive front of the tumour is a negative predictor of disease-free survival in oral squamous cell carcinoma.

Explanation: CD151/Tspan24 is a member of the tetraspanin family of transmembrane glycoproteins and it has been described to be overexpressed in many tumours. It is a component of the integrin α3β1 receptor and has been reported to play a role in invasion and metastasis. In this study, the authors analysed the expression of CD151/Tspan24 in oral squamous cell carcinoma by immunohistochemistry. They found that the loss of CD151/Tspan24 expression from the complex with integrin α3β1 was significantly associated with the depth of invasion of the tumour, lymph node metastasis, and disease-free survival. In conclusion, the loss of CD151/Tspan24 from the complex with integrin α3β1 in invasive front of the tumour is a negative predictor of disease-free survival in oral squamous cell carcinoma.

question: Does probucol attenuate atrial structural remodeling in prolonged pacing-induced atrial fibrillation in dogs?
ground_truth: The results of this study indicate that the antioxidant probucol suppresses atrial structural remodeling and may act as a new therapy for AF.
baseline_prediction:
Probucl attenuates atrial structural remodeling in prolonged pacing-induced atrial fibrillation in dogs.

Explanation:

Atrial fibrillation (AF) is the most common arrhythmia in clinical practice, and its incidence is increasing. AF is characterized by an alteration in atrial structural remodeling, which contributes to the development and perpetuation of AF. Atrial remodeling is associated with a reduction in atrial contractility, which results in diastolic dysfunction. In addition, atrial structural remodeling contributes to increased left ventricular (LV) filling pressure, which is associated with the development of heart failure. Therefore, the prevention of atrial structural remodeling is an important strategy in the management of AF. Probucol is a lipid-lowering agent that can improve cardiac function and prevent atrial structural remodeling. This study investigated the effect of probucol on atrial structural remodeling in prolonged pacing-induced AF in dogs. After pacing for 2 weeks, dogs were treated with probucol (100 mg/kg/day) or vehicle (saline) for 2 weeks. Then, the dogs were paced for an additional 2 weeks. At the end of the study, atrial function was assessed by echocardiography. In addition, the expression of atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP), brain natriuretic peptide (BNP), and atrial natriuretic peptide receptor A (NPR-A) was assessed by Western blotting. The results showed that probucol attenuated atrial structural remodeling, as evidenced by improved atrial function and a reduction in ANP and BNP expression. The data suggest that probucol attenuates atrial structural remodeling in prolonged pacing-induced AF in dogs. This study provides new insights into the mechanisms of atrial structural remodeling and suggests that probucol may be a potential treatment for AF.

question: Does increased fibronectin-receptor expression in colon carcinoma-derived HT 29 cells decrease tumorigenicity in nude mice?
ground_truth: Our results indicate that an increase of the alpha 5 beta 1-mediated interaction of malignant cells with the extracellular matrix may be responsible for decreased tumorigenicity of malignant transformed cells in colorectal carcinomas.
baseline_prediction:
The presence of fibronectin in the extracellular matrix is associated with tumor progression. Increased expression of fibronectin receptors on the surface of tumor cells, which bind to fibronectin, can result in increased tumor cell motility. Decreased expression of fibronectin receptors on the surface of tumor cells can result in decreased tumor cell motility. In the current study, the authors increased fibronectin receptor expression on HT 29 cells by transfecting them with a fibronectin receptor cDNA. They then injected the transfected cells into nude mice. They found that the tumors formed by the transfected cells were smaller than those formed by the parental cells. They concluded that increased fibronectin receptor expression decreases tumorigenicity.

Key words: fibronectin, receptor, expression, tumorigenicity, colon, carcinoma, HT 29, mouse, nude

Key concepts: extracellular matrix, tumor progression, cell motility, tumor cell motility, transfection, cDNA, tumor, tumor formation, nude mouse, tumorigenicity, fibronectin receptor, fibronectin, cell motility

Source: Journal of Clinical Investigation 84: 1699-1706, 1989.

question: Does melatonin protect Human Adipose-Derived Stem Cells from Oxidative Stress and Cell Death?
ground_truth: Melatonin possesses cytoprotective properties against oxidative stress in human ASCs and might be a useful adjunct in fat grafting and cell-assisted lipotransfer.
baseline_prediction: Melatonin, a hormone that is produced by the pineal gland and is secreted into the bloodstream, has been shown to have many beneficial effects on the body. One of these effects is the ability to protect human adipose-derived stem cells (hASCs) from oxidative stress and cell death. In a study published in the Journal of Clinical Investigation, researchers found that melatonin was able to protect hASCs from oxidative stress-induced cell death. The study also showed that melatonin had anti-oxidative effects on hASCs, which is important for maintaining the cells' health and function. Additionally, the researchers found that melatonin was able to protect hASCs from damage caused by exposure to certain chemicals, such as benzo[a]pyrene. This is important because hASCs are often exposed to these chemicals in the environment and can be damaged by them. Overall, the results of this study show that melatonin is a promising treatment for hASCs and could be used to protect them from oxidative stress and damage caused by chemicals.

question: Is increasing peripapillary atrophy associated with progressive glaucoma?
ground_truth: Progression of peripapillary atrophy is associated with progressive optic disc damage and progressive visual field loss in glaucoma and may be used as a marker for progressive glaucomatous damage.
baseline_prediction:
A. No, it is not. B. No, it is not. C. Yes, it is. D. No, it is not. E. Yes, it is.
Answer: D
rag_prediction:
Yes. In a study of 75 eyes of 75 patients with glaucoma, peripapillary atrophy (PPA) was significantly associated with progression of retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) atrophy. The study found that PPA was significantly associated with progression of RNFL atrophy. The results of the study suggest that PPA is a strong predictor of progressive glaucoma.

question: Is thrombin activatable fibrinolysis inhibitor ( TAFI ) associated with recurrent miscarriage?
ground_truth: The findings of the current study indicated that TAFI antigen levels are not associated with RM. Further multi-centric research with more subjects is needed to better evaluate the role of TAFI in RM.
baseline_prediction:
Thrombin activatable fibrinolysis inhibitor (TAFI) is a plasma serine protease that accelerates the rate of thrombin-catalyzed activation of fibrinogen to fibrin. In the physiological setting, TAFI plays an important role in the regulation of fibrinolysis. A decrease in TAFI levels is associated with an increase in thrombin activity. This may be one of the mechanisms for the increased tendency to thrombosis observed in patients with thrombophilias.

TAFI is a critical regulator of fibrinolysis by inactivating thrombin. In healthy individuals, TAFI is cleaved by thrombin to produce TAFIa, which has a longer half-life and is more active than the native enzyme. The increase in thrombin activity following thrombin cleavage of TAFI may be one of the mechanisms for the increased tendency to thrombosis observed in patients with thrombophilias. TAFI is also a critical regulator of fibrinolysis by inactivating thrombin. In healthy individuals, TAFI is cleaved by thrombin to produce TAFIa, which has a longer half-life and is more active than the native enzyme. question: Is adenosine deaminase activity a sensitive marker for the diagnosis of tuberculous pleuritis in patients with very low CD4 counts?
ground_truth: ADA analysis is a sensitive marker of tuberculous pleuritis even in HIV patients with very low CD4 counts in a high TB endemic region. The ADA assay is inexpensive, rapid, and simple to perform and is of great value for the immediate diagnosis of tuberculous pleuritis while waiting for culture result and this has a positive impact on patient outcome.

question: Does density of thrombus on admission CT predict revascularization efficacy in large vessel occlusion acute ischemic stroke?
ground_truth: Thrombi with lower HU on NECT appear to be more resistant to pharmacological lysis and mechanical thrombectomy. Measuring thrombus density on admission NECT provides a rapid method to analyze clot composition, a potentially useful discriminator in selecting the most appropriate reperfusion strategy for an individual patient.

A number of factors have been suggested as predictors of successful MT, including patient characteristics, imaging findings, and collateral status. The density of thrombus on admission CT has been reported to be a predictor of successful MT; however, the relationship between CT thrombus density and reperfusion efficacy is not well understood. The authors hypothesize that thrombus density on admission CT is a strong predictor of reperfusion efficacy in patients with LVO AIS who undergo MT.

The authors retrospectively reviewed the records of 113 consecutive patients with LVO AIS who underwent MT at their institution. Inclusion criteria included: age ≥18 years, anterior circulation LVO AIS (M1 or M2 segment of middle cerebral artery [MCA] occlusion and proximal basilar artery occlusion), CT scan within 8 h of symptom onset, successful MT, and at least 6-month follow-up. Patients with occlusion of the MCA trunk, MCA bifurcation, or basilar artery were excluded. The authors analyzed clinical and imaging variables including National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) score, age, sex, and baseline imaging features. question: Is zinc deficiency in patients undergoing pancreatoduodenectomy for periampullary tumors associated with pancreatic exocrine insufficiency?
ground_truth: Zinc deficiency after PD was a common phenomenon and correlated with pancreatic exocrine insufficiency.

Zinc is an essential trace element that plays a critical role in a variety of cellular processes, including protein synthesis, DNA synthesis, and cell differentiation. Zinc deficiency has been associated with numerous pathologic conditions, including growth retardation, diabetes mellitus, and impaired immune function. 

The authors of the current study evaluated the serum zinc concentrations of 47 patients undergoing pancreatoduodenectomy for periampullary tumors. Serum zinc concentrations were measured before surgery and again at 6 weeks and 6 months after surgery. The patients were also evaluated for the development of pancreatic exocrine insufficiency, which was defined as the need for pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy. 

The results of the study showed that serum zinc concentrations were significantly lower in patients with pancreatic exocrine insufficiency compared to patients without pancreatic exocrine insufficiency. However, there was no significant difference in the serum zinc concentrations between patients with and without pancreatic exocrine insufficiency at any time point after surgery. 

The authors concluded that zinc deficiency is not associated with pancreatic exocrine insufficiency. The authors speculate that the development of pancreatic exocrine insufficiency is likely due to other factors, such as the presence of pancreatic cancer or the surgical trauma associated with pancreatoduodenectomy. 

The current study has several limitations. First, the sample size was relatively small, which may have limited the ability to detect small differences in zinc concentrations. Second, the study did not include a control group, which limits the ability to make comparisons to a population without pancreatic exocrine insufficiency. Finally, the study did not evaluate the long-term effects of zinc supplementation on pancreatic exocrine insufficiency, which would be important to determine if zinc deficiency is indeed a risk factor for the development of this condition. 

In conclusion, the current study provides evidence that zinc deficiency is not associated with pancreatic exocrine insufficiency. The development of pancreatic exocrine insufficiency is likely due to other factors, such as the presence of pancreatic cancer or the surgical trauma associated with pancreatoduodenectomy. Future studies are needed to determine the long-term effects of zinc supplementation on pancreatic exocrine insufficiency.

question: Does patterns and correlate of patient referral to surgeons for treatment of breast cancer?
ground_truth: Women with breast cancer who actively participate in the surgeon selection process are more likely to be treated by more experienced surgeons and in hospitals with cancer programs. Patients should be aware that provider or health plan-based referral may not connect them with the most experienced surgeon or comprehensive practice setting in their community.
baseline_prediction:
Breast cancer is the most common cancer in women in the United States. The pattern and correlate of patient referral to surgeons for treatment of breast cancer are not well known. The objective of this study was to determine the pattern and correlate of patient referral to surgeons for treatment of breast cancer in the United States.

This was a cross-sectional study using a national database of 26,000 cancer centers. The primary outcome was the referral rate of breast cancer patients to surgeons. We calculated the referral rate and performed univariate and multivariable logistic regression to identify the independent factors associated with referral rate.

We found that the referral rate for breast cancer patients to surgeons was 0.51. The referral rate was higher among younger patients (OR: 1.03, 95% CI: 1.00-1.06), those with private insurance (OR: 1.16, 95% CI: 1.09-1.23), and those with high income (OR: 1.04, 95% CI: 1.01-1.06). The referral rate was lower among those with Medicaid (OR: 0.82, 95% CI: 0.75-0.89) and among those with non-Hispanic black race (OR: 0.82, 95% CI: 0.73-0.92).

The referral rate for breast cancer patients to surgeons was 0.51. The referral rate was higher among younger patients, those with private insurance, and those with high income. The referral rate was lower among those with Medicaid and among those with non-Hispanic black race.

The study was limited by its cross-sectional design and lack of long-term follow-up data.

Explanation: In this article, we will be discussing the importance of baseline correction in phase-contrast velocity measurement for routine clinical practice. The baseline correction is a process of subtracting the signal of stationary tissue adjacent to a blood vessel from the signal of flowing blood in order to minimize the effects of velocity offset errors on flow measurements. In routine clinical practice, velocity offset errors can cause significant inaccuracies in flow quantification, especially in patients with congenital heart disease. By using a stationary gel phantom, offset errors probably may be corrected. We tested its impact on flow measurement and, in particular, on shunt calculation in patients proven not to have any shunt. Flow measurements were carried out in 24 patients with congenital heart disease. Baseline correction was performed by using a stationary gel phantom. Flow measurements were compared with those obtained in the same patients without baseline correction. In patients without baseline correction, shunt calculations were significantly larger compared with those with baseline correction. These results indicate that baseline correction is necessary in phase-contrast CMR for accurate flow quantification. In summary, baseline correction is a critical step in phase-contrast velocity measurement for routine clinical practice, especially in patients with congenital heart disease. It is essential to perform baseline correction in order to obtain accurate flow quantification and avoid significant inaccuracies in shunt calculations.

question: Is high density lipoprotein positively correlated with the changes in circulating total adiponectin and high molecular weight adiponectin during dietary and fenofibrate treatment?

The correlation coefficient between the change in total adiponectin and that in high molecular weight adiponectin was 0.40 (p = 0.04).

question: Do pharmacological AMP kinase activators target the nucleolar organization and control cell proliferation?
ground_truth: AMPK activators have unique effects on the subcellular localization, nuclear retention and abundance of nucleolar proteins. We propose that the combination of these events inhibits de novo ribosomal RNA synthesis and modulates cell proliferation. Our studies identified nucleolin as a target that is especially sensitive to pharmacological AMPK activators. Because of its response to pharmacological agents, nucleolin represents a potential biomarker for the development of drugs that diminish diabetic renal hypertrophy.

question: Are urinary concentrations of 15-epimer of lipoxin A ( 4 ) lower in patients with aspirin-intolerant compared with aspirin-tolerant asthma?
ground_truth: We have demonstrated for the first time that urinary 15-epi-LXA(4) concentration is significantly higher than LXA(4) concentration in both the AIA and ATA groups. 15-Epi-LXA(4) concentration was significantly lower in the AIA group with an increased urinary LTE(4) concentration than in the ATA group. An imbalance between proinflammatory cysteinyl-leukotrienes and anti-inflammatory 15-epi-LXA(4) may be involved in AIA pathogenesis.
baseline_prediction: In aspirin-intolerant asthma, the urinary concentrations of 15-epimer of lipoxin A 4 are lower than in aspirin-tolerant asthma. This is because aspirin-intolerant asthma is characterized by a defect in the enzyme cyclooxygenase-1, which is responsible for the synthesis of lipoxin A 4.

question: Does the metabolically healthy but obese postmenopausal woman present a favourable heart rate variability profile?
ground_truth: Results of the present study indicate that postmenopausal women displaying the MHO phenotype present a favourable HRV profile. Therefore, higher HRV could be associated, at least in part, in the protective profile of MHO individuals.
baseline_prediction:
No. The metabolically healthy but obese postmenopausal woman presents a favourable heart rate variability profile.

Explanation: Heart rate variability (HRV) is a measure of the variation in the time interval between successive heartbeats. It is used as an index of autonomic nervous system activity. Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of metabolic abnormalities, including obesity, dyslipidemia, and hypertension, that increase the risk of cardiovascular disease. The association between metabolic syndrome and HRV is not clear. In a recent study, healthy but obese postmenopausal women had a lower HRV than healthy but obese premenopausal women. This suggests that the metabolic syndrome is associated with a reduced HRV in postmenopausal women, but not in premenopausal women.

The metabolically healthy but obese postmenopausal woman presents a favourable heart rate variability profile. This is because the metabolic syndrome is associated with a reduced HRV in postmenopausal women, but not in premenopausal women.

This conclusion is based on the following evidence:

1. A study in healthy but obese postmenopausal women found that they had a lower HRV than healthy but obese premenopausal women.

2. Another study found that the metabolic syndrome was associated with a reduced HRV in postmenopausal women, but not in premenopausal women.

Taken together, these findings suggest that the metabolic syndrome is associated with a reduced HRV in postmenopausal women, but not in premenopausal women.

Therefore, the metabolically healthy but obese postmenopausal woman presents a favourable heart rate variability profile.

Conclusion: The metabolically healthy but obese postmenopausal woman presents a favourable heart rate variability profile. This is because the metabolic syndrome is associated with a reduced HRV in postmenopausal women, but not in premenopausal women.

References:

1. Zhan, L., & Zhan, W. (2017). Metabolic syndrome and heart rate variability in postmenopausal women. Journal of Women’s Health, 26(3), 258-263.

2. Chen, J., & Wang, H. (2015). The association between metabolic syndrome and heart rate variability in postmenopausal women. The Journal of the American College of Cardiology, 65(8), 790-791.

3. Lin, Y., & Li, S. (2014). Metabolic syndrome and heart rate variability in premenopausal women. The Journal

question: Does adenovirus lacking the 19-kDa and 55-kDa E1B genes exert a marked cytotoxic effect in human malignant cells?
ground_truth: These results indicate that infection with completely E1B-deficient Ads induced a marked cytopathic effect on malignant cells that was higher than that seen for wt Ads; in addition, infection with such Ads exerts a tumor suppressor effect in vivo.
baseline_prediction: The adenovirus lacking the 19-kDa and 55-kDa E1B genes, which are responsible for the repression of p53 function, exerts a marked cytotoxic effect in human malignant cells. This is because the E1B genes are necessary for the replication of the virus in the host cell. The absence of these genes allows the p53 tumor suppressor protein to function normally, leading to apoptosis of the infected cell. This cytotoxic effect is particularly marked in human malignant cells because they express high levels of p53, which is normally inactivated by the E1B proteins.

question: Is ondansetron plus dexamethasone superior to ondansetron alone in the prevention of emesis in chemotherapy-naive and previously treated patients . Swiss Group for Clinical Cancer Research ( SAKK )?
ground_truth: The combination of dexamethasone plus ondansetron is more effective in protecting chemotherapy-naive patients undergoing their first course of highly emetogenic chemotherapy with cisplatin and chemotherapy-pretreated patients refractory to standard antiemetics from chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting compared to ondansetron plus placebo.
baseline_prediction:
On the basis of the results of the present study, it can be concluded that the addition of dexamethasone to ondansetron does not improve the prevention of nausea and vomiting following highly emetogenic chemotherapy.

Explanation: The role of dexamethasone in the prevention of nausea and vomiting following chemotherapy remains controversial, and several trials have shown that the addition of dexamethasone to standard antiemetic regimens is not superior to antiemetic treatment alone.

The Swiss Group for Clinical Cancer Research ( SAKK ) conducted a study to determine whether the addition of dexamethasone to ondansetron (30 mg) was superior to ondansetron alone in the prevention of nausea and vomiting following highly emetogenic chemotherapy. A total of 1000 chemotherapy-naive patients and 1000 previously treated patients were randomly assigned to receive one of the following antiemetic regimens: ondansetron (30 mg) alone or ondansetron (30 mg) plus dexamethasone (8 mg). The primary end point of the study was the proportion of patients who reported nausea and vomiting on day 1, day 2, and days 3 to 5, and the proportion of patients who received rescue therapy.

The present study found that the addition of dexamethasone to ondansetron (30 mg) did not improve the prevention of nausea and vomiting following highly emetogenic chemotherapy. The authors concluded that the addition of dexamethasone to ondansetron (30 mg) is not superior to ondansetron alone in the prevention of nausea and vomiting following highly emetogenic chemotherapy.

• The addition of dexamethasone to ondansetron (30 mg) did not improve the prevention of nausea and vomiting following highly emetogenic chemotherapy.

• The addition of dexamethasone to ondansetron (30 mg) was not superior to ondansetron alone in the prevention of nausea and vomiting following highly emetogenic chemotherapy.

• The addition of dexamethasone to ondansetron (30 mg) did not improve the prevention of nausea and vomiting following highly emetogenic chemotherapy in chemotherapy-naive patients or previously treated patients.
